The Engineered Solutions segment, for instance, saw revenue dip from $76.6 million in the second quarter of 2024 to $70.3 million at the same time in 2025. According to management, this is because of fewer business wins and less cross selling efforts that could be blamed on slower demand from existing customers. This naturally brought with it a decline in profits from $14.9 million to $12.7 million. And for the first half of the year, the decline in revenue from $150.9 million to $136.2 million brought with it a small drop in segment profits from $25.8 million to $24.4 million.

Lastly, we have the Nissens Automotive segment. This is the newest segment that the company has, set up as a result of its purchase of Nissens Automotive. Naturally, the timing of the deal means that the segment generated nothing in the way of revenue or profit in the second quarter of last year. That means nothing for the first half of the year as well. But for the current fiscal year, revenue came in at $90.5 million. From this, the company generated segment profits of $35.2 million. For the first half of this year as a whole, segment profits amounted to $58.4 million from $156.7 million worth of revenue.

Overall, I would say that things are going quite well. As for the current fiscal year in its entirety, management mentioned that revenue should grow at a low 20% rate. They also expect an EBITDA margin of between 10% and 11%. If we assume that revenue grows at exactly 21% and we take the midpoint of guidance for the EBITDA margin, we would get EBITDA for this year of $184.4 million. This would likely translate to adjusted operating cash flow of about $116.1 million.

If we use these estimates, and we also use the results that the company achieved for the 2024 fiscal year, we can see in the chart above how shares are currently priced. These are multiples that, to me at least, look appealing. But in addition to the stock being cheap on an absolute basis, it's also cheap compared to most other similar firms. In the table below, I stacked it up against five such businesses. And what I found was that, on a price-to-operating-cash-flow basis, two of the five firms were cheaper than it is. And when it comes to the EV-to-EBITDA approach, only one of the companies is cheaper than it is.

Company Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
Standard Motor Products 7.5 7.9
American Axle & Manufacturing Holdings (AXL) 1.7 4.1
Patrick Industries (PATK) 10.3 11.0
XPEL Inc. (XPEL) 15.8 11.9
Holley (HLLY) 11.2 8.1
LCI Industries (LCII) 7.2 9.3

Even though I have been correct about the direction the stock would experience, this does not mean that I was correct across the board. In my previous article, I talked about car pricing and inventory trends. My general conclusion was that depressed industry inventories for new vehicles especially, but to a lesser extent involving used vehicles as well, would lead to higher prices. In the chart below, you can see the days supply of new and used vehicles in the space.

In the chart below, you can now see the average listing price of new cars versus used cars. I had predicted, based on inventory trends, that prices for both types of vehicles, but especially for new ones, would create a favorable environment for Standard Motor Products since higher new car prices would likely cause consumers to keep their used cars longer. And since there is a huge focus from Standard Motor Products in the aftermarket space, I thought that this would mean a positive outlook for the business. As the aforementioned chart shows, however, you can see that the premium of new cars over used cars really has not changed over the last few months. And with new inventories having risen, I don't really see much of a chance of that premium widening in the near term.

Takeaway

Based on all the data currently available, I believe that the market continues to underestimate Standard Motor Products. The company is a solid business that has done a fine job as of late. It is possible that we could see some volatility in the near term. But for those focused on the long haul, I think that maintaining it as a "Buy" candidate is the right choice.

data.title: RTX: Load Up For A 'Safe' Defense Play
data.description:
Record breaking defense budgets, increased dependence on national security, and next-generation military technologies, are all leading to signs that the aerospace and defense sector is a literal “safe” place to be for investors. RTX Corp (NYSE:RTX) is emerging as a big name in the industry.

The company provides systems and services for the commercial, military, and government customers in the United States and global, with three main segments. Collins Aerospace, offering defense products and military aircraft service solutions. Pratt & Whitney, which supplies aircraft engines and commercial auxiliary power units. Then lastly, Raytheon, which provides threat detection, tracking, and mitigation, often for the government.

RTX is up 42% YTD and has gained nearly 2.5x since the fall of 2023. There is a lot that I like about RTX and the industry, and I give this stock a buy rating. Their backlog has been accelerating, and missile demand is just heating up. They just inked a nice deal with their radar systems, and are sneakily valued at more of a discount than the eye can see.

Guidance & Backlog

All three segments for RTX saw strong organic growth in Q2, with Pratt & Whitney leading the charge at 12% organic sales growth. Engine shipments to customers continues to grow and the order backlog is now at almost 1,100 orders and commitments for the first half of 2025. Collins Aerospace and Pratt & Whitney received an updated higher forecast in adjusted and organic sales for the remainder of 2025, while Raytheon remained the same.

RTX now has $236 billion in total backlog, split between $144 billion in commercial contracts (engine parts), and $92 billion in defense programs (missiles, radar). This massive backlog should help fund the continued innovation this industry is seeing, while providing great shareholder returns, like their newly increased dividend that is up 8% to 1.67%.

Missile Demand

Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth has stated that the U.S. defense department wants to see missile production happen at a faster rate, to make sure the stockpile of artillery is ready and large for any future conflicts.

RTX has already been prepping with factory expansion and added workers looking to stock up that inventory. A $115 million expansion of its Redstone Missile Integration in Huntsville, Alabama has been in the process for over a year and will eventually allow for a ramp up in production capacity. They also recently received a $1.1 billion contract in Tucson, Arizona for increased production of the AIM-9x Sidewinder missile, which supports the U.S. Navy, Army, and Air Force.

New Radar Deal

Just a few days ago, it was reported that RTX agreed on a deal worth $1.7 billion to supply Lower Tier Air and Missile Defense Sensor (LTAMDS) radar systems for the U.S. Army and Poland. The deal is huge for RTX on multiple fronts. It marks Poland as the first international customer to use their advanced 360-degree LTAMDS radar as a part of their defense regime. Hopefully it will open doors to other countries and allow for scalability in their Raytheon segment. Work will be done in Massachusetts and the contract has a face value of $3.79 billion. Threat detection has become vital, and RTX just managed a huge win in the military radar world.

Valuation Misleading

On the grading scale, the biggest stand out from a negative side would come from the stock’s valuation. Forward P/E is 34.65, up from the sector average of 23x. Price to sales and price to book are only slightly elevated, just above average. There are two other main reasons why this valuation doesn’t leave me fearful.

RTX supports a PEG of 0.2, 6x lower than the sector average. Growth has been consistent and reliable, and based on guidance and industry strength, the trend is very likely to continue. Earnings for RTX have dependably risen year after year, and net income is $6.15 billion for the last twelve months, almost double what their 2023 full year result was.

Also, in November of 2023, right as RTX began its climb, its P/E ratio was 38, even higher than where it’s at today two years later. If valuation can remain and actually even fall slightly while the stock more than doubles, I think that is a very good sign.

On top of that, the 34.65 forward P/E is not crazy high by any means compared to the broader market average, and the stock’s 50-day moving average is widening the gap further from its 200-day with nice momentum.

Risk to Thesis

RTX slightly lowered their EPS forecast for the remainder of 2025, with management noting that tariff impacts will still be felt. The company initially expected an $850 million hit to its profit back in April when the trade war had escalated. More recently in July, it was that the impact is anticipated to be closer to $500 million for the year. No doubt, a headwind and tough burden on the firm, but at least the impact expectation has been lowered. Management has alluded to tariff constraints on the bottom line, but continue to seek strategic global operation opportunities to mitigate where they can.

Conclusion

There really isn’t much not to like for RTX. Tariffs are their biggest risk. They will look to ramp up production in the U.S. where they can, especially for the new deals they just locked in. Growth in the defense industry is just getting started as countries around the world load up on inventory and expand defense budgets. They are well versed across aerospace and defense products, parts, systems, missiles, radars, and more. Backlog is growing, and valuation is better than it appears when you look at future growth and historical ratios. Load up on RTX for a "safe" investment.

data.title: First Industrial: Tariff Fears Are Overblown
data.description:
Shares of First Industrial Realty (NYSE:FR) have been a poor performer over the past year, losing about 7% of their value. Its warehouse business has exposure to international trade flows, given its large West Coast presence, and shares have only recovered a portion of their April lows amidst fears tariffs could structurally suppress trade levels. I last covered shares in June, rating the stock a “Buy,” but it has continued to lag, gaining just 4% while the market has added 12%. With updated financials and the Fed cutting rates, now is a good time to see if underperformance is set to continue or if FR can recover.

West Coast exposure is a concern

First Industrial is a leading owner of industrial warehouses across the United States. It has, what has historically been, an excellent footprint, focused near key ports and transit hubs. This has led to consistently strong demand for its space as importers/exporters lease warehouses to store their inventory before distribution across the country. As you can see, it has an outsized exposure to the West Coast ports, which have boomed over the past 25 years as trade with China has grown. While the company has been expanding into Florida and Phoenix to diversify its business, the West Coast is still clearly the most important region for the country, and this exposure has been a source of concern given President Trump’s trade policies.

Trade has been better than feared

Still, it is important to highlight that despite the significant shift in trade policy, we have not seen trade levels fall as much as one might have expected, at least not yet. As you can see below, goods imports were actually up 1.4% year over year in July. There was a massive surge in Q1 of this year as companies sought to pull-forward imports to avoid tariffs, but even with this slowdown, we have not yet flipped negative.

In fairness, Q2 tariff rates were lower, as there was an interim 10% tariff during negotiations. Now, many key imports have tariff rates in the 15%-25% range, so we may see imports decelerate more. Plus, to the extent that tariffs encourage re-shoring, it takes time to stand up domestic production capacity. As such, I do anticipate that imports persistently grow more slowly than the U.S. economy, though it does not appear that a meaningful contraction in international trade is likely as a result of tariffs.

Admittedly, while that is a positive for First Industrial, I would note that trade with China has indeed plunged. Imports broke below their COVID lows as tariff rates briefly surpassed 100%. With tariff rates now 55%, we have seen some rebound in activity, but imports from China are depressed and likely to remain so absent a deal with the country, which does not appear imminent. Now, to the extent imports from China are replaced with imports from Vietnam, Thailand, or India, FR’s business is unimpacted. But if production is moved domestically or to Mexico, that likely would reduce the flow of goods through its West Coast-oriented warehouses.

Results remain strong

Thus far, tariffs are not causing imports to roll over in a threatening way, and frankly, First Industrial continues to perform well, even as the glut of new construction has clearly pressured occupancy. In the company’s second quarter, reported on July 16, First Industrial generated $0.76 of FFO, which beat expectations by $0.03 as revenue grew by 10% to $180 million. Same-store net operating income was up 8.7% given higher lease rates, partially offset by a tick down in occupancy.

The company did report 94.2% Q2 occupancy, down 1.1 percentage points from a year ago. This drop is partially due to recent new builds that have now entered service and start at a lower occupancy, and as these fill up, occupancy should tick higher, and management expects to end the year at about 95%-96% occupancy. Still, there has definitely been a decline in occupancy as new construction completes and demand softens a bit.

Industrywide, First Industrial sees vacancy running at 6.3%, as disclosed on the earnings call. As you can see below, occupancy is running lower than during the post-COVID boom as e-commerce and goods consumption exploded. Importantly, FR’s occupancy is better than the industry, and the company continues to enjoy very strong renewal activity, even as it has slowed from a record high. 2.5 million sq ft of leases commenced in the quarter. Cash rental rate increases were 33%, and it continues to expect 30%-40% cash rental growth. These renewals continue to power FFO growth.

Supply can offset softer demand

Additionally, while demand has softened, supply is also coming down, which should help to tighten the market. New construction is down 70% with the amount of space currently under construction down over 50%. With less supply coming to the market, it is easier to maintain occupancy even at a lower level of demand. We are now through the worst of the completion activity, and I expect that to help sustain a ~95% occupancy rate next year.

FR’s own development activity is also relatively modest. It has $285 million of development activity, which is 39% leased. It typically builds speculatively rather than building to order, which increases the risk but enhances the return, assuming it successfully leases the space. It sees a cash yield of 7.2% on these projects. This pipeline is validated by recent success. Most notably, it has been able to fully lease out its 1.8 million sq ft space in Phoenix. Additionally, construction costs are down 5%-10% on new builds from a year ago, but it wants to see more consistent demand before accelerating development activity. Lower costs help to protect economics even at lower rental rates. Offsetting some of this, tariffs could be a ~1%-2% cost increase on construction, which is still fairly manageable.

With a high-quality balance sheet and dividend coverage, FR is attractive

Importantly, First Industrial maintains an excellent balance sheet with 4.5x debt to EBITDA. In fact, the company enjoyed a credit rating upgrade to BBB+ last quarter. It also has no maturities before 2027, protecting it from elevated rates. Beyond a conservative balance sheet, it also has a diversified business as its top 20 tenants are just 26% of net rent.

Back in June, I was targeting $2.80-$2.90 in FFO. Alongside Q2 results, it tightened its guidance range to a midpoint of $2.92. I have been conservative in my assumptions about lease timing on new builds given tariff uncertainty. I now see the company generating $2.85-$2.93. I remain a bit more cautious just given lingering tariff pressure.

Shares currently offer a 3.4% dividend yield, and the company maintains a very conservative dividend coverage ratio of 1.63x. I view ~1.4x coverage as the sustainable long-term level, giving FR ~$2.07 of distribution capacity. That would support a 16% increase in the dividend. In practice, what I expect FR to do is grow its dividend faster than FFO and gradually bring down its coverage ratio. For instance, even at just 3% FFO growth, it can grow its dividend by 8% per year through 2028 before hitting the 1.4x level. In practice, given embedded rent increases from renewals, I see FFO growing closer to 5% even with flat occupancy, suggesting 8% dividend growth for ~6 years.

While tariffs have clearly weighed on sentiment, I view FR as attractive given its potential for high-single-digit dividend growth through 2030 even in a modest growth environment, thanks to its strong balance sheet and excellent coverage. Additionally, with supply-side pressures fading, I believe markets are focused too much on demand-side pressure, and I recommend continuing to "Buy" shares and accumulate a position in FR.
